story:
The old Victorian on Pictor Street breathes with secrets. Night after night, the mayor’s widow climbs to the attic and burns letters in a ritual of smoke and shame. Forgive me. I had no choice. They’re watching. Ash drifts over the floorboards; rust-brown crescents stain her cuffs. Absolution never comes.

After the mayor’s “accidental” fall from his office balcony, men in uniform ask about a missing courier. The papers praise the mayor; the widow serves tea and trembles prettily. But there were boot prints on the railing too large for him, and there was a courier who died quietly off the record. From his stiff hand she took a silver heart locket engraved A.M. and tucked it beneath a loose floorboard by the hearth alongside a single ledger page stamped with the mayor’s seal: $250,000, offshore account #4492-771. She traces the numbers at night and whispers, Stupid boy. You should’ve taken the money.

A journalist arrives—Matt Roberts, Daily Sentinel—smelling of ink and certainty. He says he’s writing about the mayor’s legacy, then asks about a courier rumored to be carrying proof of embezzlement. She smiles, pours bourbon, and keeps the curtains shut tight. Men never look down: not at the shifted rug, not at the floorboard with a telltale gap.

That night, the clock wheezes past three. She pries up the board, palms the locket, and slips out into the moon-bleached garden. Under the oldest hydrangea, she has already buried one mistake. She digs, ready to hide the last piece that could chain her to it. A twig snaps. Across the garden stands Roberts, camera lifted.

Click.

Thunder shakes the house at dawn. The widow powders bruises she’s painted on and rehearses her line: A robbery. He broke in. I… had no choice. When Roberts returns that evening for “a letter from your husband,” she leads him to a forged page and swings a fireplace poker like a bat. He folds, bleeding, and grins through it: Too late. The photo’s already uploaded. They grapple—flash, silk, panic—until her hand finds the pearl-handled gun.

Bang.

By noon, a detective in a wet trench coat is circling the parlor. The widow clutches lace and sorrow, repeating her robbery story. He notes the overturned chair, the shattered camera, the dark stain that won’t hide under wool. At the window he pauses. Odd place for a security camera, he says, tapping the glass. Motion-activated, night vision, audio. Installed by the mayor, before his death. To monitor you. The mayor didn’t trust you, did he?

Her composure cracks. The detective opens the casing and plucks out the memory card. He also has the locket—A.M.—dangling from his glove. Everything has been seen, and heard. The widow is led out in handcuffs as rain presses the hydrangeas toward the earth, washing mud from roots like knuckles.

The house goes quiet. The last ashes stir in the attic draft. The true witness—red light steady through nights of garden burials, parlor whispers, and lies—has already spoken.

Click. The light blinks once, twice, three times, and goes dark.
